lesson_title: 13.5 — Introduction to overloading the I/O operators

cards.text: To enable std::cout << myEnum; to print enum names, you can {{c1::overload}} the {{c2::insertion operator}} ({{c3::<<}}).
cards.source: 13.5 — Introduction to overloading the I/O operators

cards.front: What are the insertion and extraction operators for I/O?
cards.back:
Insertion operator: << (output to stream)
Extraction operator: >> (input from stream)
cards.source: 13.5 — Introduction to overloading the I/O operators

cards.question: What does this function signature enable?
cards.answer: Enables outputting Color enums with std::cout (e.g., std::cout << myColor).
cards.explanation: Overloads the << operator for Color type, allowing custom output behavior.
cards.source: 13.5 — Introduction to overloading the I/O operators

cards.text: The signature for overloading output operator is: {{c1::std::ostream&}} operator<<({{c2::std::ostream&}} out, {{c3::YourType}} obj);
cards.source: 13.5 — Introduction to overloading the I/O operators

cards.front: Why does operator<< overload return std::ostream&?
cards.back:
To enable chaining: std::cout << x << y << z;
Returning the stream allows the next << to use the result.
cards.source: 13.5 — Introduction to overloading the I/O operators

cards.question: What does this implementation do?
cards.answer: Overloads << to output Color enums as their string names instead of integers.
cards.explanation: Combines switch-based enum-to-string with operator overloading for clean syntax.
cards.source: 13.5 — Introduction to overloading the I/O operators

cards.text: To overload the input operator: {{c1::std::istream&}} operator>>({{c2::std::istream&}} in, {{c3::YourType&}} obj);
cards.source: 13.5 — Introduction to overloading the I/O operators

cards.front: Why is the second parameter of operator>> a reference?
cards.back: The extracted value needs to be stored in the original variable, so it must be passed by reference to allow modification.
cards.source: 13.5 — Introduction to overloading the I/O operators

cards.concept: Overloading I/O operators for custom types
cards.explanation:
Benefits of overloading << and >>:
  • Cleaner syntax than named functions
  • Consistent with built-in types
  • Enables chaining operations
  • Makes custom types feel "native"
Use for frequently printed/input types.
examples: std::cout << a << b << c;  // Chaining works
cards.source: 13.5 — Introduction to overloading the I/O operators

cards.front: Where should you typically define operator<< and operator>> for a type?
cards.back: In the same namespace as the type, as non-member functions. This allows argument-dependent lookup (ADL) to find them.
cards.source: 13.5 — Introduction to overloading the I/O operators

cards.text: To overload output for custom types: std::ostream{{c1::&}} operator{{c2::<<}}(std::ostream{{c3::&}} out, MyType obj);
cards.source: 13.5 — Introduction to overloading the I/O operators

cards.text: Overloaded I/O operators return {{c1::stream reference}} to enable {{c2::chaining}}: cout << a << b << c;
cards.source: 13.5 — Introduction to overloading the I/O operators

cards.text: To overload output for custom types: std::ostream{{c1::&}} operator{{c2::<<}}(std::ostream{{c3::&}} out, MyType obj);
cards.source: 13.5 — Introduction to overloading the I/O operators
